text: Good evening. Let's look at your day.
How would you describe today in one word?
You said '{A1_OPEN.answer}'. When something went well today, what made it happen?
You said '{A1_OPEN.answer}'. When things got difficult, what was your first instinct?
options: Figure out what I could control
Wait for someone to step in
answer=I prepared well|I adapted on the fly:A1_R_INT
answer=The team came through|I got lucky:A1_R_EXT_LIGHT
answer=Figure out what I could control|Push through alone:A1_R_INT_LIGHT
answer=Wait for someone to step in|Feel stuck:A1_R_EXT

text: You see your hand in what happened today. That's pure agency. You stayed in the driver's seat.
You recognize external factors helped you today. Humility is good, but don't forget your own effort.
Even in difficulty, you looked for what you could do. That's a strong internal locus of control.
A tough day pulls attention outward. But somewhere in there, you made a choice. Remember that you always have agency.
Now let's shift from how you handled things — to what you gave.
Think about one interaction you had today. Were you giving or expecting?

options: answer=I helped someone|I taught someone something:A2_Q_CONTRIB

text: When you helped out, how did it feel?
options: I hoped it would be noticed

text: When you felt that way, what was your core expectation?
options: I expected others to do their part
I felt the system was unfair
answer=Draining|I hoped it would be noticed:A2_R_ENTITLE_LIGHT
answer=I deserved more recognition|I expected others to do their part|I felt the system was unfair:A2_R_ENTITLE

text: True organizational citizenship happens when giving feels like its own reward. You contributed strongly today.
It's natural to want recognition for extra effort. Just ensure your primary drive remains the work itself.
Needing support is human. Acknowledging it is the first step to building strong reciprocal relationships.
When we focus heavily on what we are owed, we give away our power to focus on what we can give.
Finally, let's look at the scope of your focus today.
When you think about today's biggest challenge, who comes to mind?
options: Just me — it was my problem
My team — we were all affected
answer=Just me — it was my problem:A3_Q_SELF
answer=My team — we were all affected|A specific colleague|The customer or end user:A3_Q_ALTRO

text: Focusing inward helps us survive. But what would happen if you widened your lens to how this challenge affects others?
options: I'd see how my work helps the bigger picture
It feels like too much responsibility
I just want to get my part done

text: You framed the challenge around others. Does thinking about them make the work feel heavier or lighter?
options: Heavier, it's a lot of pressure
answer=I'd feel less alone|I'd see how my work helps the bigger picture:A3_R_ALTRO_LIGHT
answer=It feels like too much responsibility|I just want to get my part done:A3_R_SELF
answer=Lighter, it gives it meaning|It grounds me in reality:A3_R_ALTRO
answer=Heavier, it's a lot of pressure|It makes me anxious:A3_R_SELF_LIGHT

text: Opening up your radius of concern can actually relieve stress by connecting you to a shared purpose.
Self-preservation is a strong instinct. But remaining self-centric can sometimes amplify our own pain. Try to look outwards tomorrow.
That's self-transcendence. Finding meaning in serving others is a profound source of resilience.
Empathy can be heavy. Make sure to balance caring for others with healthy boundaries for yourself.
Today your locus of control leaned {axis1}. Your orientation was more towards {axis2}. And your radius of concern leaned {axis3}. Thank you for reflecting.
See you tomorrow.
